text.title: Focus Group Structure, Composition, and Moderation Rules
text.summary: When focus groups beat interviews, group composition rules, discussion guide structure, and moderation techniques.

rule.statement: Use focus groups for attitude exploration, concept reactions, language discovery, and cross-segment comparison. Do not use them for usability testing, sensitive topics, individual behavior measurement, or final go/no-go decisions.
rule.rationale: Group dynamics are a feature for attitude exploration (diverse reactions surface quickly) and a bug for usability testing (participants suppress struggle when observed by peers). The method matches some research goals and actively harms others.

rule.statement: Run minimum 2-3 groups per segment. Never report a finding from a single group.
rule.rationale: A single group finding is anecdote, not pattern. Group composition, moderator dynamics, and random participant selection create too much session-level noise for any single group to be representative.

rule.statement: 6-10 participants per group. Homogeneous enough for comfort (common frame of reference), diverse enough for variety (different perspectives). Run 2-4 groups total.
rule.rationale: Below 6: insufficient dynamic diversity. Above 10: moderator cannot manage all voices; quieter participants get lost. Homogeneity enables candor; diversity enables range.

rule.statement: Screen aggressively for professional respondents (people who cycle across studies). Use live screener calls or short video tasks to verify participation quality.
rule.rationale: Professional respondents give polished, rehearsed answers that do not represent real users. They are common on large panel platforms and inflate perceived consensus.

section.title: Discussion Guide Structure
section.p: Standard 90-min structure: Opening 5 min (introductions, ground rules) → Warm-up 10 min (easy questions) → Core topics 40-60 min (3 topics maximum) → Concept reaction 15 min (if showing stimuli) → Wrap-up 5 min (summary, final thoughts).

rule.statement: Show concepts and stimuli only after the warm-up and initial topic exploration. Introducing stimuli too early biases the discussion toward reacting to your framing rather than expressing independent attitudes.
rule.rationale: Pre-stimulus exploration captures the user's unprompted mental model. Introducing stimuli first anchors all subsequent discussion to the product frame, suppressing organic vocabulary and unprompted concerns.

rule.statement: Capture written first-impressions (silent post-it round) before group discussion to break anchoring on the first speaker's view. Then open the floor.
rule.rationale: The first speaker's view anchors the whole group. Written capture before discussion preserves independent assessments that would otherwise be lost to social conformity.
